rubric_results.requirement: Getting Started Guide exists and is complete
rubric_results.evidence: docs/quickstart.md is a comprehensive getting-started guide that takes users from install (curl one-liner, line 7-8) through trying a registry flow instantly (lines 13-27), creating their own flow from scratch with full YAML walkthrough (lines 29-80), running it in three modes — headless, --watch UI, and --report (lines 82-106), adding loops with exit rules (lines 108-139), adding human gates (lines 141-157), and calling flows from agents via --wait (lines 159-173). The guide closes with navigation links to deeper docs. All six new-user rubric items (NU1-NU6) passed, confirming the guide is sufficient for a new user to create, validate, and run a flow.

rubric_results.requirement: REST API endpoints are documented (at least /api/jobs, /api/flows, /api/health)
rubric_results.evidence: docs/api.md (508 lines) is a thorough API reference. Jobs section (lines 11-183): list, create, get, start, pause/resume, cancel, delete, tree, runs, rerun, events, context injection — all with HTTP method, path, query params, request/response examples. Runs section (lines 187-280): fulfill, step-events, cost, cancel, agent-output with event type table. Engine section (lines 282-322): tick, status (GET /api/status serves as health check, returns active_jobs/total_jobs/registered_executors), executors. Templates CRUD (lines 324-357). Configuration (lines 361-416): get, models, update models, set API key. WebSocket protocol (lines 418-473) with tick and agent_output message formats plus JS connection example. Error responses (lines 477-492) with status code table. Environment variables (lines 494-507). Also notes Swagger UI at /docs (line 7) for interactive exploration.

rubric_results.requirement: Complete reference for flow YAML syntax (steps, inputs, outputs, exits, config, for-each)
rubric_results.evidence: docs/flow-reference.md (700 lines) is an exhaustive YAML reference covering: flow directory format, complete structure with all top-level fields, all 5 executor types with config tables and examples — script, external, poll, llm, agent — session continuity, inputs including optional and any_of syntax, exit rules with expression namespace and examples, loop patterns — self-loop and multi-step, for-each fan-out/fan-in, flow steps / sub-flow composition, conditional branching with when + any_of merge, prompt templating, step limits, idempotency modes, decorators — timeout, retry, notification, fallback, common patterns including post-loop gating, 17 documented gotchas, config variables with resolution priority, requirements, and validation checklist. Additionally, docs/yaml-format.md (607 lines) provides a complementary reference with data model mapping table.

rubric_results.requirement: Common error messages are documented with resolution steps
rubric_results.evidence: No dedicated error messages reference exists. Partial coverage is scattered across multiple files: docs/agent-integration.md has a 4-item troubleshooting section (lines 352-358) covering 'Missing required input', 'Timeout on external step', 'Empty outputs array', and 'Job not found' with brief resolutions. docs/cli.md documents exit codes (lines 32-49) for both standard and --wait modes. docs/api.md covers HTTP error response format with status codes 400/404/500 (lines 487-492). docs/flow-reference.md has 17 gotchas describing common authoring mistakes. The validate command output is shown in docs/cli.md but doesn't enumerate the specific errors and warnings the validator can produce. Missing entirely: a systematic reference for common engine runtime errors (cycle detection failures, artifact validation mismatches, exit rule evaluation errors, cost/duration limit exceeded, session health failures), YAML parse errors, and validator warnings with causes and resolution steps.

rubric_results.requirement: High-level architecture (engine, executors, store, server) is documented
rubric_results.evidence: docs/concepts.md provides a complete conceptual architecture: quick reference table mapping all core concepts, Jobs with lifecycle and sub-job spawning, Job Staging with STAGED lifecycle and groups and dependencies and data wiring, Steps as pure-function typed nodes, all 5 Executor types with comparison table, Dependencies — input bindings for data flow and after for ordering with parallel execution, Exit Rules and Loops with action table and supersession mechanism, For-Each with sub-job model, Conditional Branching with when/any_of/settlement, Handoff Envelopes as step contract, Observability — web UI and reports and SQLite access, Shell Hooks vs Server Notifications, and Flows as Tools with 5 interaction modes. docs/why-stepwise.md complements with design philosophy, comparison to CrewAI/AutoGen/Temporal/LangGraph, and 6 design principles.

qualitative_summary: Stepwise's documentation is strong for 1.0 readiness across four of five evaluated dimensions. The quickstart (docs/quickstart.md) takes users from install to a running flow in under 5 minutes, progressively introducing executors, loops, human gates, and agent integration. The API reference (docs/api.md) is thorough with request/response examples for every endpoint group plus WebSocket protocol documentation. The YAML reference (docs/flow-reference.md at 700 lines + docs/yaml-format.md at 607 lines) is exceptionally complete — covering all executor types, input binding variants, exit rules, for-each, conditional branching, decorators, config variables, requirements, and 17 documented gotchas. The conceptual architecture (docs/concepts.md) gives users a solid mental model of jobs, steps, executors, and the DAG execution model, with docs/why-stepwise.md providing design rationale. The primary gap is the absence of an error messages reference — users encountering engine errors, validator warnings, or executor failures have no centralized guide for diagnosis and resolution. The scattered troubleshooting in agent-integration.md covers only 4 agent-caller scenarios. A dedicated errors/troubleshooting page documenting common validate warnings, engine error categories (cycle detection, artifact validation, exit rule evaluation), and executor failure modes with resolution steps would close this gap and improve the new-user experience when things go wrong. The new-user simulation's confusion points — all eventually resolved — suggest the quickstart could also benefit from a simpler 2-step script-only hello-world before the 4-step code-review example, but this is polish rather than a blocker.
